era_context: Late Renaissance transitioning to early modern period: the Mughal Empire under Akbar is at its height; the Ming Dynasty in China is in the Wanli Emperor's reign; in Europe, the late Renaissance is giving way to early modern culture; the Ottoman Empire remains powerful; the Nine Years' War in Ireland is in its final phase; Japan is transitioning from the Sengoku period to the Edo period.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (minimal European documentation; indigenous records sparse)
Pacific Islands and Oceania (virtually no contemporary European contact recorded)
Interior of the Americas (limited European penetration; indigenous records not systematically preserved in European sources)

events.title: Edict of Nantes
events.description: King Henry IV of France issued the Edict of Nantes on 13 April 1598 (OS) / 30 April 1598 (NS), granting limited toleration to Huguenots (French Protestants) and ending the Wars of Religion that had devastated France for nearly 40 years. The edict granted Huguenots freedom of conscience and limited freedom of worship, though it maintained Catholicism as the state religion. The Edict of Nantes became a landmark in the history of religious toleration and remained in effect until its revocation in 1685. The edict marked the end of the Wars of Religion and the beginning of a period of relative religious peace in France.

sources.name: French royal records and contemporary accounts

events.certainty_note: The Edict of Nantes is one of the most well-documented events of this year. The date is precisely attested in multiple sources.
cross_references: Precedes revocation of Edict of Nantes (1685)

events.title: Death of Toyotomi Hideyoshi
events.description: Toyotomi Hideyoshi, the great unifier of Japan who had ended the Sengoku period of civil war, died on 18 September 1598 (Gregorian calendar). Hideyoshi had been the second great unifier of Japan, following Oda Nobunaga. His death left power in the hands of a regency council, which was quickly dominated by Tokugawa Ieyasu. Hideyoshi's young son, Toyotomi Hideyori, was nominally the heir, but Ieyasu maneuvered to consolidate power, leading to the establishment of the Tokugawa shogunate in 1603. Hideyoshi's death marked the beginning of the end of the Toyotomi clan's dominance and the rise of the Tokugawa.

sources.name: Tokugawa Iemitsu's records and later Edo-period chronicles

events.certainty_note: Hideyoshi's death is well-documented in Japanese historical records. The date (18 September 1598) is precisely attested.

events.title: Treaty of Vervins
events.description: The Treaty of Vervins, signed on 2 May 1598, ended the Franco-Spanish War (1595–1598), which had been fought over control of territories in the Low Countries and France. The treaty was signed between King Henry IV of France and King Philip II of Spain. The treaty confirmed French possession of territories gained during the war and established a period of peace between France and Spain. The Treaty of Vervins, combined with the Edict of Nantes signed just weeks earlier, marked the end of a period of religious and political turmoil in France and the beginning of a period of relative stability.
events.certainty_note: The Treaty of Vervins is one of the most well-documented events of this year. The date is precisely attested in multiple sources.

events.title: Philip II of Spain's Death
events.description: King Philip II of Spain died on 13 September 1598 at the Escorial Palace. Philip II had been one of the most powerful monarchs of the 16th century, ruling Spain, the Spanish Netherlands, and vast colonial territories in the Americas and Asia. His death marked the end of an era of Spanish dominance and the beginning of the decline of Spanish power in Europe. Philip II was succeeded by his son, Philip III, who would face increasing challenges from France, England, and the Dutch Republic. Philip II's death also marked the end of the Franco-Spanish War, as the Treaty of Vervins had been signed just weeks earlier.
key_figures: Cardinal Duke of Lerma (regent and advisor)

sources.name: Spanish royal records and contemporary accounts
Historiographic consensus in Spanish and European history

events.certainty_note: Philip II's death is one of the most well-documented events of this year. The date (13 September 1598) is precisely attested.

events.title: Akbar's Reign at Its Height
events.description: The year 1598 marks the height of the Mughal Empire under Emperor Akbar, who had ruled since 1556. Akbar had consolidated Mughal power over most of the Indian subcontinent and was known for his administrative reforms, religious tolerance, and cultural patronage. In 1598, Akbar was in his late 50s and would begin his final military campaigns in the Deccan in 1599. The year 1598 represents the peak of Mughal power and stability, before the challenges that would emerge after Akbar's death in 1605.
events.certainty_note: Akbar's reign and its height in 1598 are well-documented in Mughal records and later histories.

events.title: Ming Dynasty's Wanli Emperor in Mid-Reign
events.description: The year 1598 falls in the reign of the Wanli Emperor (1572–1620), who had ruled the Ming Dynasty for 26 years. The Wanli Emperor's reign was marked by relative stability but also by increasing fiscal strain and factional conflict at court. By 1598, the emperor was becoming increasingly withdrawn from court affairs, delegating power to eunuchs and officials. The year 1598 represents a period of consolidation in Ming rule, before the challenges that would emerge in the early 17th century and contribute to the dynasty's eventual fall in 1644.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming)

events.certainty_note: The Wanli Emperor's reign and the state of the Ming Dynasty in 1598 are well-documented in Chinese records.

disconfirming_evidence: The Edict of Nantes is sometimes presented as a triumph of religious tolerance, but modern historians note that it was a pragmatic compromise that left significant restrictions on Huguenot rights and was always intended to be temporary. The treaty was revoked in 1685, and many Huguenots were persecuted or forced to emigrate. The significance of Philip II's death has been somewhat overstated in popular histories; while it marked the end of an era, Spanish power remained formidable under Philip III, and Spain continued to be a major European power for another century. The Wanli Emperor's reign is sometimes presented as a period of decline, but modern historians note that it was a period of relative stability and cultural flourishing, with the decline accelerating only after his death.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1598 is rich for Europe and Asia, with particular strength in political and military history. The sources are predominantly court-sponsored chronicles, state papers, and later compilations. There is significant bias toward elite perspectives and toward literate, centralized states (France, Spain, Mughal Empire, Ming China, Japan). Indigenous American, African, and Oceanic histories are almost entirely absent from contemporary European sources. The year is well-documented for major political and military events but poorly documented for economic, social, and cultural developments outside elite circles. Oral traditions and non-written records from non-literate societies are almost entirely lost.

graph_edges.note: Edict of Nantes in 1598 was revoked in 1685
Death of Toyotomi Hideyoshi in 1598 led to Tokugawa Ieyasu's rise to power in 1600
Treaty of Vervins in 1598 was signed shortly before Philip II's death
Philip II's death in 1598 led to reign of Philip III (1598–1621)
Akbar's reign at its height in 1598 led to Deccan campaigns in 1599
